otherSide: A functionalist might argue that the mandate meaningfully regulates interstate commerce in fresh produce and health services, that the Sebelius inactivity doctrine is an artificial limitation that ignores cumulative economic effects, and that the fee can be characterized as a tax if structured as income-based.

title: Federalizing Domestic Violence in Public Housing: Category 3 and the Substantial Effects Test
doctrineArea: Commerce Clause - Category 3 Substantial Effects
description: Congress enacts the Domestic Violence Prevention and Housing Act, which criminalizes domestic violence occurring in any federally-financed residential unit, including public housing projects and private buildings that receive any federal housing subsidies or tax benefits. The statute applies a strict liability standard and includes federal penalties up to 15 years imprisonment for each offense. A defendant accused of a single incident of domestic violence occurring in a subsidized apartment moves to dismiss, arguing that Congress lacks constitutional authority because domestic violence is non-economic, intrastate activity that falls outside the commerce power. The government responds that the statute regulates activity substantially affecting interstate commerce in federally-financed housing and related insurance markets.

steps.title: Economic Activity and Aggregation Under Raich and Wickard
steps.prompt: Is domestic violence in public housing 'economic activity' susceptible to aggregation under Raich v. Gonzales and Wickard v. Filburn?

choices.text: No. Domestic violence is inherently non-economic, personal conduct. Unlike home-grown marijuana in Raich (which was part of an economic market for controlled substances) or wheat in Wickard (an agricultural commodity), domestic violence does not constitute economic activity at all. Without underlying economic activity, aggregation analysis does not apply, and Congress cannot rely on the substantial effects test.
choices.explanation: In Raich, the Supreme Court permitted aggregation of home-grown marijuana because, even though the individual plaintiff's conduct was purely personal, marijuana growing was part of a larger economic market (the illicit drug trade), and aggregating all such activity showed substantial effects on interstate commerce. Wickard aggregated individual farming decisions. Here, the domestic violence itself is not an economic activity and is not part of any economic market. Unlike drugs or agriculture, domestic violence does not have an economic character even when aggregated. This distinction suggests Lopez Category 3 does not apply unless domestic violence can be connected to an economic market.

choices.text: Yes. Under Raich, even non-economic, intrastate personal conduct can be regulated if it substantially affects interstate commerce. Domestic violence in federally-financed housing affects insurance markets, real estate values, and housing supply, so it constitutes activity substantially affecting interstate commerce and is susceptible to aggregation.
choices.explanation: This answer misapplies Raich. While Raich did permit regulation of non-commercial marijuana cultivation, it did so because cultivation was part of an economic market (drugs). Raich does not stand for the proposition that any intrastate, non-economic personal conduct may be regulated if it has some economic effect. Domestic violence, standing alone, is not economic activity. The fact that it affects insurance or real estate markets does not transform the violent conduct itself into economic activity. Morrison v. United States rejected precisely this broad aggregation theory.

choices.text: Yes. Wickard establishes that Congress can aggregate any conduct affecting interstate commerce, regardless of economic character. Because aggregate domestic violence incidents affect housing markets, it is susceptible to aggregation.
choices.explanation: Wickard permits aggregation only of economic activity. While Wickard involved agriculture, a clearly economic domain, subsequent cases have clarified that the aggregation principle applies to economic conduct. Morrison explicitly rejected the government's attempt to aggregate non-economic gender-motivated violence based on aggregate effects on interstate commerce. The same principle applies here: aggregating non-economic violent conduct does not convert it into regulable economic activity.

choices.text: No. Because the statute is enforced by the federal government (not state government), it exceeds the commerce power regardless of whether domestic violence is economic activity.
choices.explanation: This answer confuses federalism with the commerce power. Federal enforcement does not inherently exceed congressional authority; the question is whether the regulated conduct is within the commerce power. The distinction between state and federal enforcement relates to principles of federalism and the Tenth Amendment, not to whether an activity is economic or substantially affects interstate commerce. A federal statute can validly regulate activity within the commerce power.

steps.title: Four-Part Lopez Category 3 Analysis
steps.prompt: Applying the four-part Category 3 framework (jurisdictional element, economic character, essential nexus to regulated activity, and catch-all substantial effects test), does the statute survive constitutional scrutiny?

choices.text: No. While the statute includes a jurisdictional element (federal housing nexus) and does involve an economic scheme (federally-financed housing), domestic violence is not economic activity, and the statute lacks the essential nexus to a broader economic regulation present in cases like Wickard and Raich. The statute fails because it attempts to regulate non-economic personal violence, not economic activity affecting interstate commerce.
choices.explanation: Under Lopez's framework: (1) the statute has a jurisdictional element (federal housing), (2) it purports to regulate activity affecting interstate commerce (housing and insurance markets), but (3) the critical deficiency is that domestic violence is non-economic activity, and (4) the statute cannot satisfy the substantial effects test because the underlying conduct is not economic. Morrison established that Congress cannot aggregate non-economic conduct based on aggregate economic effects. The federal housing nexus is not sufficient to transform personal violence into regulable economic activity. The statute fails the essential nexus requirement.

choices.text: Yes. The statute survives because it includes a clear jurisdictional element (the federal housing nexus), satisfies the substantial effects test (domestic violence affects insurance and housing markets), is part of a broader economic scheme (federal housing), and therefore falls within Category 3.
choices.explanation: This answer overlooks the essential requirement that Category 3 applies to economic activity. While the statute has a jurisdictional element and relates to an economic scheme (housing), the regulated conduct itself (domestic violence) is non-economic personal conduct. Morrison teaches that a nexus to economic activity is insufficient if the target of regulation is non-economic. The federal housing connection does not transform the violence itself into economic activity subject to aggregation.

choices.text: Yes. Because the statute is 'essential to a larger economic regulation' (federal housing regulation), it passes Category 3 scrutiny under the rationale of Raich, which permitted regulation of non-commercial activity that is part of a broader regulatory scheme.
choices.explanation: This answer misinterprets Raich's 'essential to a larger scheme' requirement. In Raich, the non-commercial home cultivation was essential to a larger economic regulation (the drug market). Here, domestic violence is not essential to regulating housing markets; it is peripheral and incidental. The statute does not directly regulate the housing market; it attempts to criminalize personal violence that occurs in federally-subsidized units. The necessary connection to the economic scheme is attenuated.

choices.text: No. The statute violates the doctrine of qualified immunity because it imposes federal criminal liability for state law crimes.
choices.explanation: Qualified immunity applies to civil rights suits against government officials, not to the constitutionality of federal criminal statutes. This answer confuses doctrine areas. The constitutional question is whether Congress has enumerated power to criminalize domestic violence under the commerce power, not whether prosecutors have immunity. The federalism concern is real but is addressed through the commerce power analysis, not qualified immunity.

steps.title: Morrison's Non-Economic Violence Doctrine
steps.prompt: How does United States v. Morrison's reasoning about gender-motivated violence inform the analysis of this statute?

choices.text: Morrison holds that Congress cannot regulate non-economic violence based on aggregate effects on interstate commerce, even if that violence substantially affects interstate markets. Morrison directly invalidates statutes criminalizing gender-motivated violence as beyond the commerce power. This statute, which criminalizes domestic violence (a form of gender-motivated violence), similarly regulates non-economic personal conduct and fails under Morrison's holding.
choices.explanation: In Morrison, the Supreme Court struck down the civil rights remedy of the Violence Against Women Act, holding that gender-motivated violence is not economic activity and that Congress cannot regulate such violence under the commerce power despite substantial aggregate effects on interstate commerce. Morrison rejected the government's argument that violence affecting healthcare, insurance, and commerce markets constitutes regulable economic activity. This statute faces the identical constitutional barrier: it targets non-economic personal violence. The addition of a federal housing nexus does not distinguish this case from Morrison because the nexus relates to the venue, not the economic character of the violence itself.

choices.text: Morrison is inapplicable because that case involved a civil remedy, not federal criminal law. The commerce power extends further to federal crimes than to federal civil rights statutes.
choices.explanation: Morrison explicitly holds that violence is non-economic activity beyond the commerce power, regardless of whether Congress exercises this authority through criminal or civil remedies. The opinion does not carve out an exception for federal criminal law. If anything, federal criminal statutes must satisfy the same enumerated powers limitations as civil statutes. The remedy type does not affect the underlying commerce power analysis.

choices.text: Morrison is distinguishable because the VAWA provision at issue in Morrison lacked a jurisdictional element. This statute includes a federal housing nexus, which Morrison implicitly permits under Category 3.
choices.explanation: Morrison's holding is not limited to statutes lacking jurisdictional elements. The Court's concern in Morrison was that the underlying conduct (gender-motivated violence) is non-economic, not that the statute lacked sufficient jurisdictional specificity. Many statutes have jurisdictional elements requiring a connection to interstate commerce, but Morrison teaches that such elements cannot redeem a statute that targets non-economic conduct. The federal housing nexus provides jurisdictional specificity but does not cure the fundamental flaw.

choices.text: Morrison established that gender-motivated violence is a type of economic activity affecting interstate commerce. This statute therefore falls squarely within Congress's commerce power.
choices.explanation: This answer inverts Morrison's holding. Morrison explicitly held that gender-motivated violence is non-economic activity. While Morrison acknowledged that such violence has substantial aggregate effects on interstate markets, the Court rejected the argument that these effects convert the violence into regulable economic activity. Morrison is famous precisely for its holding that non-economic conduct falls outside the commerce power despite economic effects.

overallExplanation: This scenario tests deep understanding of Lopez's four-part Category 3 framework, the distinction between economic and non-economic activity, the aggregation limits of Wickard and Raich, and the Morrison doctrine prohibiting regulation of non-economic violence. It requires students to see how Morrison, despite its age, remains the controlling precedent limiting commerce power over personal violence.
professorNote: United States v. Morrison (2000) addresses federal violence-against-women laws. Despite extensive congressional findings of aggregate effect of gender-motivated violence on interstate commerce, Court struck down provision. Confirms Lopez was not anomaly. Key holdings: (1) non-economic activity (violence) cannot be aggregated; (2) activity is not essential to larger economic regulatory scheme; (3) causal chain to commerce too attenuated; (4) allowing federal regulation intrudes on traditional state police power over violent crime. Morrison holds that even detailed congressional findings linking activity to commerce cannot overcome structural limits on Commerce Clause power.
otherSide: A functionalist approach might emphasize that domestic violence has substantial aggregate economic effects on interstate insurance and housing markets, that modern commerce power jurisprudence permits regulation of activities substantially affecting interstate commerce, and that the federal housing nexus represents a reasonable jurisdictional hook for federal authority.

title: The Children's Digital Safety Fee: Tax or Penalty?
doctrineArea: Taxing Power - Tax vs. Penalty
description: Congress enacts the Children's Digital Safety Act, which requires social media platforms to verify the age of all users under 16 before allowing them access. Platforms that fail to verify face a '$5,000 compliance fee' per unverified minor account. The statute imposes strict liability (no scienter requirement), and the aggregate fees are capped at 2 percent of a platform's gross revenue. The fee is collected by the IRS and deposited into the General Treasury. Congress justifies the fee as a regulatory mechanism to protect children's privacy and safety, with the revenue raised being secondary to the regulatory purpose.

steps.title: Sebelius Tax-Penalty Factors
steps.prompt: Under NFIB v. Sebelius's factors for distinguishing a tax from a penalty, how should courts analyze the '$5,000 per account' compliance fee?

choices.text: The fee should be characterized as a penalty, not a tax. The fee is triggered exclusively by violation of a regulatory mandate (failure to verify age), the amount ($5,000) is substantial relative to the cost of compliance, and the statute imposes strict liability without scienter. Under Sebelius, these factors indicate the fee is primarily punitive rather than a constitutional exercise of taxing power.
choices.explanation: Sebelius identifies multiple indicia of a penalty: (1) the levy is triggered exclusively by conduct Congress deems unlawful (non-compliance), (2) the amount is substantial relative to the cost of compliance, and (3) the statute imposes strict liability without scienter. Here, all three factors favor the penalty characterization. In contrast, taxes are typically low-rate, broadly applicable, and do not require intent. The $5,000 per unverified account exceeds reasonable compliance costs and appears designed as punishment. The Sebelius framework weighs heavily toward penalty characterization.

choices.text: The fee should be characterized as a valid tax under Sebelius. Because the statute's primary purpose is regulatory (protecting children), not revenue-raising, and because the fee is capped at 2% of revenue, Sebelius permits this fee as a valid exercise of taxing power.
choices.explanation: Sebelius's test is not whether the primary purpose is regulatory; it is whether the payment meets the objective criteria of a tax (low rate, broad application, income-based or similarly neutral criteria, lack of scienter). The statutory purpose of regulation does not convert a penalty into a tax. The ACA individual mandate was upheld partly because the penalty was low (capped at 2.5% of income) and income-indexed; here, a $5,000 flat fee is neither low nor income-sensitive. The 2% revenue cap is a reasonable limitation but does not address the fundamental problem that the per-account structure resembles a penalty.

choices.text: The fee is a tax because Congress labeled it a 'fee' rather than a 'penalty,' and the IRS collects it. Under Sebelius, form controls over substance when revenue is involved.
choices.explanation: Sebelius explicitly rejects form-over-substance analysis. The Chief Justice stated that courts must examine the payment's functional characteristics, not its label or collection mechanism. Calling something a 'fee' does not make it a tax, and IRS collection does not automatically render a payment a valid exercise of taxing power. Sebelius's entire framework rests on substance over form, requiring courts to look at triggering events, scienter, and amount relative to compliance costs.

choices.text: The fee is neither a tax nor a penalty but a valid regulatory license fee. Congress can impose license fees on businesses to fund regulatory programs, and Sebelius does not apply to licensing schemes.
choices.explanation: While business licensing fees are a recognized form of revenue, this statute is not structured as a traditional license fee. License fees are typically flat or predictable amounts associated with the privilege of engaging in licensed activity. This fee is triggered exclusively by non-compliance with a mandate, is per-violation, and is substantial. It functions as punishment for non-compliance, not as a fee for the privilege of operating a platform. Sebelius's framework would apply to distinguish this fee from a legitimate business license.

steps.title: Per-Account Structure vs. Individual Mandate
steps.prompt: Does the per-account fee structure (applied to platforms, not individuals) change the Sebelius analysis compared to the ACA's individual mandate (applied to persons)?

choices.text: No. While the fee is imposed on platforms rather than individuals, the functional analysis is the same: a substantial, per-violation levy triggered exclusively by non-compliance with a regulatory mandate. The Sebelius factors apply regardless of who is charged, and the per-account structure actually strengthens the penalty characterization because it is more nakedly tied to violation of the mandate than an individual income-based penalty would be.
choices.explanation: Sebelius's tax-penalty framework focuses on the functional character of the payment, not the identity of the payer. Whether imposed on individuals or entities, a substantial, violation-triggered levy without scienter looks like a penalty. If anything, the per-account structure—directly counting non-compliance and imposing a per-incident charge—more clearly resembles traditional penalties than the ACA's individual mandate, which was imposed at the individual level and was income-indexed. The fact that platforms, not individuals, face the levy does not rescue the fee's characterization under Sebelius.

choices.text: Yes. Because the fee is imposed on business entities rather than individuals, Sebelius does not apply. Sebelius regulated individual conduct, but this statute regulates business conduct, so a different constitutional framework applies and the fee is valid.
choices.explanation: Sebelius's tax-penalty framework is not limited to individual conduct. The logic of the framework—distinguishing payments that function as taxes from payments that function as penalties—applies equally to payments imposed on businesses. Many constitutional doctrines apply to both individuals and entities. The fact that platforms rather than individuals are charged does not exempt the statute from Sebelius scrutiny; it only changes the context.

choices.text: Yes. The ACA individual mandate could be upheld as a tax partly because it was income-based and applied to millions of people at a low rate. This per-account fee is much higher per occurrence, is capped at 2% of revenue, and applies only to non-compliant platforms, so it better satisfies Sebelius's tax criteria.
choices.explanation: This answer reverses the analysis. The ACA penalty was upheld as a tax precisely because it was low rate and broadly applicable. This fee is higher per occurrence (even if capped in the aggregate), narrowly triggered (only for non-compliance), and is per-violation rather than income-based. These characteristics point toward penalty, not tax. The revenue cap does not address the fundamental per-violation structure.

choices.text: Yes. Because the fee applies to business entities rather than consumers, it is a permissible exercise of the commerce power independent of the taxing power, so Sebelius does not apply.
choices.explanation: This answer confuses regulatory authority with the taxing power. Even if Congress has commerce power to regulate platforms (which is likely), that does not mean it can impose a substantial levy structured as a penalty without relying on the taxing power. If the fee is a penalty-like levy, it must be justified as a tax under the Sixteenth Amendment and Article I, Section 8. Regulatory authority over platforms does not create a separate avenue for imposing violation-triggered fees that circumvent Sebelius's framework.

overallExplanation: This scenario tests application of the Sebelius tax-penalty framework to a modern regulatory context. It requires students to assess whether a compliance fee meets Sebelius's objective criteria (rate, trigger, scienter, breadth) and to apply the framework beyond the specific context of health insurance to business regulation.
caseReferences: Bailey v. Drexel Furniture Co., 259 U.S. 20 (1922)
professorNote: Tax vs. Penalty analysis applied to social media fee. Factors: (1) Collecting agency—collected by IRS or state tax agency favors tax; regulatory agency favors penalty. (2) Relation to taxable income—if calculated based on income or financial metrics, favors tax. (3) Scienter—intent requirement favors penalty; taxes impose no scienter. (4) Amount—modest amount permitting rational choice to pay or comply favors tax; excessive burden disfavoring compliance favors penalty. Overall: primarily raise revenue (tax) or primarily regulate behavior (penalty)? Fee structured as tax may be valid under taxing power; if structured as regulatory penalty, requires Commerce Clause or other independent basis.
otherSide: A functionalist might argue that the regulatory purpose is clear, the fee is reasonable relative to costs, the 2% revenue cap makes it genuinely revenue-neutral, and platforms have compliance alternatives, suggesting the fee is valid as a tax incentive for regulatory compliance.

title: The AI Oversight Board and the Legislative Veto Problem
doctrineArea: Separation of Powers - Legislative Veto and Bicameralism
description: Congress creates a Federal AI Oversight Board, a multi-member independent agency empowered to issue binding rules regulating AI development, training, and deployment. The Board is composed of five members appointed by the President with Senate confirmation. However, any Board rule can be nullified within 90 days of issuance if both Houses of Congress pass a concurrent resolution of disapproval, without presentation to the President for signature or veto. The concurrent resolution procedure requires only passage by majority vote in each chamber, with no Presidential action. An AI company's rule challenged before an administrative law judge challenges the Board's authority to issue rules subject to such a legislative veto mechanism.

steps.title: INS v. Chadha and the Legislative Veto
steps.prompt: Under INS v. Chadha, does the concurrent resolution mechanism violate constitutional requirements of bicameralism and presentment?

choices.text: Yes. The concurrent resolution mechanism violates INS v. Chadha's holding that legislative action must satisfy bicameralism (passage by both chambers) and presentment (submission to the President). Here, while the mechanism does satisfy bicameralism, it bypasses presentment by operating without Presidential action. This one-House veto was the precise mechanism struck down in Chadha, and a two-House veto without presentment is equally unconstitutional.
choices.explanation: In INS v. Chadha, the Supreme Court struck down a one-House legislative veto as violating bicameralism and presentment requirements. The principle is not specific to one-House vetoes; it is that any 'legislative action' must satisfy Article I's procedures. A concurrent resolution (two-House veto) without presentment similarly fails because it is legislative action (nullifying binding agency rules) without Presidential participation. The Constitution requires that legislative action—not merely the exercise of legislative judgment—comply with bicameralism and presentment. A concurrent resolution that nullifies rules is legislative action.

choices.text: No. The concurrent resolution mechanism does not violate Chadha because it involves both Houses (satisfying bicameralism) and therefore constitutes sufficient legislative action. Chadha struck down one-House vetoes; a two-House mechanism is constitutionally distinct.
choices.explanation: This answer misinterprets Chadha's holding. Chadha does not hold that a one-House veto is unconstitutional only because it lacks bicameralism. The Court's concern was that legislative action must comply with Article I procedures: bicameralism and presentment. A concurrent resolution without presentment violates the presentment requirement just as clearly as a one-House veto violates bicameralism. The addition of a second chamber does not cure the presentment defect. Chadha's logic extends to all legislative vetoes lacking presentment.

choices.text: No. The mechanism is constitutional because the Board itself is an agency exercising executive power, and Congress can impose conditions on the exercise of delegated power. The concurrent resolution does not exercise legislative power; it merely conditions the Board's authority.
choices.explanation: This answer mischaracterizes the concurrent resolution as a condition rather than legislative action. When Congress passes a resolution nullifying a binding rule, it is exercising legislative power—the power to make law. The fact that the rule originates from an agency does not change the character of a resolution nullifying it. Chadha explicitly held that legislative action affecting legal rights and obligations requires bicameralism and presentment, regardless of whether it responds to agency action. Conditioning delegated authority does not permit bypassing constitutional procedures.

choices.text: No. The mechanism is constitutional because it includes a 90-day waiting period, allowing time for consideration. This procedural safeguard addresses Chadha's concerns about expedited action.
choices.explanation: The 90-day waiting period does not address the constitutional defect. Chadha's concern was not about speed; it was about the requirement that legislative action satisfy bicameralism and presentment. A 90-day period does not cure the absence of Presidential presentment. The temporal feature is irrelevant to the constitutional structure. The defect is structural, not procedural.

steps.title: Report-and-Wait Alternative
steps.prompt: Would a 'report-and-wait' provision be constitutionally preferable? Suppose the statute required the Board to submit rules to Congress and wait 60 days before the rule takes effect, allowing Congress to pass legislation disapproving the rule by bicameral enactment presented to the President.

choices.text: Yes. A report-and-wait provision followed by traditional legislative disapproval would be constitutional. It would allow Congress to disapprove by passing a disapproval bill subject to bicameralism and presentment, ensuring the President can veto disapproving legislation. This satisfies Chadha because Congress is not acting legislatively through concurrent resolution; it is exercising its traditional legislative power through enactment.
choices.explanation: The report-and-wait mechanism addresses Chadha's constitutional defect by requiring Congressional disapproval to follow Article I procedures. If Congress wishes to disapprove, it must pass a bill (not a concurrent resolution) that must be presented to the President. The President can veto a disapproving bill, forcing Congress to muster a supermajority. This restores the President's role in legislative action and ensures bicameralism and presentment are satisfied. This mechanism respects constitutional procedures while preserving Congressional oversight.

choices.text: No. A report-and-wait provision would still constitute a legislative veto because Congress retains the power to nullify rules. The waiting period does not change the constitutional defect; Congress is still exercising a veto.
choices.explanation: This answer conflates Congressional oversight power with legislative veto mechanisms. Congress always has the power to pass disapproving legislation; this is not a veto but the exercise of ordinary legislative power. The constitutional defect in Chadha was that Congress attempted to act legislatively without following Article I procedures (bicameralism and presentment). A report-and-wait provision that requires full legislative procedure for disapproval is constitutionally sound. The distinction is between acting legislatively without procedures (unconstitutional veto) and exercising legislative power with procedures (constitutional).

choices.text: No. A report-and-wait provision that allows Congress to disapprove by legislation violates the non-delegation doctrine because it requires Congress to engage in case-by-case review of agency rules.
choices.explanation: The non-delegation doctrine concerns whether Congress impermissibly delegates legislative authority to agencies. A report-and-wait provision does not delegate authority; it allows Congress to exercise its traditional legislative power to modify or disapprove agency rules through normal legislative procedures. Congress regularly reviews and modifies agency authority through legislation without violating non-delegation. The report-and-wait mechanism is a legitimate exercise of Congressional oversight.

choices.text: No. Any mechanism allowing Congressional disapproval is unconstitutional under Chadha, regardless of procedure.
choices.explanation: This answer over-reads Chadha. Chadha permits Congressional disapproval if done through constitutional procedures (bicameralism and presentment). The case does not preclude Congressional oversight; it requires that oversight to follow Article I's procedural requirements. Congress can legislatively disapprove agency action if it follows constitutional procedures. Chadha is a ruling about procedure, not a bar on Congressional involvement.

steps.title: Congressional Review Act and the Veto Mechanism
steps.prompt: How does the Congressional Review Act (CRA), which allows both Houses to disapprove rules through a joint resolution presented to the President, differ constitutionally from the AI Oversight Board's concurrent resolution mechanism?

choices.text: The CRA uses a joint resolution, not a concurrent resolution. A joint resolution requires bicameralism and Presidential presentment, satisfying Article I procedures. The AI Board mechanism uses a concurrent resolution, which bypasses presentment. This procedural difference is constitutionally significant under Chadha because the CRA ensures the President can veto disapproving action, whereas the Board mechanism does not.
choices.explanation: The critical constitutional difference is the presentment requirement. A joint resolution is a type of legislation that must be presented to the President; a concurrent resolution is not presented to the President. Under Chadha, legislative action must satisfy bicameralism and presentment. The CRA complies because it requires a joint resolution (presented to President), allowing Presidential veto of disapproving action. The Board mechanism bypasses presentment, violating Article I. This procedural distinction reflects the Framers' concern with Presidential participation in legislative process.

choices.text: There is no material constitutional difference. Both mechanisms allow Congressional disapproval of rules, and both operate through concurrent or joint resolutions. The labels 'concurrent' and 'joint' do not have constitutional significance.
choices.explanation: The distinction between concurrent and joint resolutions is constitutionally significant. Concurrent resolutions are not submitted to the President and do not have force of law; joint resolutions are submitted to the President and constitute legislation. Chadha turns precisely on this distinction: concurrent resolutions cannot be legislative action because they do not satisfy presentment. The CRA's use of joint resolutions ensures compliance with Article I procedures.

choices.text: The CRA is constitutional only if joint resolutions are presented to the President. But Presidential veto power over CRA disapprovals is illusory because Congress can override with supermajority. Therefore, there is no meaningful constitutional difference between the two mechanisms.
choices.explanation: The veto override possibility does not render the veto power illusory. The President's ability to veto is meaningful constitutional protection, even if Congress can override. Chadha's concern was with Presidential participation in legislative decision-making, not with whether the President's action is outcome-determinative in all cases. The veto is a constitutional check. Its overridability does not eliminate its significance.

choices.text: The AI Oversight Board mechanism is actually more constitutional than the CRA because it allows faster, more efficient rule disapproval without Presidential involvement.
choices.explanation: Chadha's holding is that Presidential involvement and Article I procedures cannot be bypassed for efficiency. The Constitution requires bicameralism and presentment precisely to slow and deliberate action, not to facilitate expedited decisions. Efficiency cannot override constitutional structure. The concurrent resolution mechanism is less constitutional, not more, because it bypasses Presidential participation.

overallExplanation: This scenario tests understanding of INS v. Chadha's holding that legislative action must satisfy bicameralism and presentment. It explores the distinction between legislative vetoes (unconstitutional) and report-and-wait procedures with full legislative disapproval (constitutional), and applies these principles to modern agency governance.
professorNote: This scenario tests whether students grasp the constitutional principle underlying Chadha—that Article I procedures are mandatory—and can apply it to variations on veto mechanisms. It also tests understanding of the CRA's constitutional features.
otherSide: A functionalist might argue that legislative vetoes provide necessary Congressional oversight of agency power, that concurrent resolutions are well-established practices, and that the formalist insistence on presentment requirements is an archaic procedural formalism that impedes efficient governance.

title: The Birth Certificate Amendment Ban: Gender Identity and the Four-Factor Suspect Classification Test
doctrineArea: Equal Protection - Gender Identity and Suspect Classification Analysis
description: A state enacts a law providing that no person may change the sex designation on their birth certificate under any circumstances, regardless of medical transition, legal recognition of gender identity in other contexts, or passage of time since transition. The statute applies prospectively to all future applications and freezes all existing birth certificates. A transgender woman who completed medical transition (including surgery), lives openly as a woman, has obtained a court order changing her legal name, and whose driver's license correctly identifies her gender, challenges the statute. She argues the law violates equal protection because it denies her the ability to amend her vital records and singles her out based on gender identity. The state defends the law as protecting the integrity of vital records and relying on immutable biological sex for administrative purposes.

steps.title: Four-Factor Suspect Classification Test
steps.prompt: Applying the four-factor test for suspect classifications (relation to ability, historical discrimination, immutability, political power), should gender identity be recognized as a suspect or quasi-suspect classification?

choices.text: Gender identity should satisfy the four factors and qualify as a suspect classification (or at minimum quasi-suspect). Gender identity: (1) typically relates to individuals' ability to function and participate in society; (2) has a history of discrimination and stigmatization; (3) is either immutable or unchangeable for practical purposes (self-identification is core to identity); and (4) is a discrete minority with limited political power. This aligns with the test for sex as a quasi-suspect classification and surpasses it in some respects.
choices.explanation: Applying the framework from United States v. Virginia and Frontiero v. Richardson, gender identity meets the suspect/quasi-suspect factors. (1) Ability: gender identity relates to fundamental aspects of personal identity and social functioning. (2) Historical discrimination: transgender individuals face documented history of legal discrimination, healthcare denial, and violence. (3) Immutability: while gender identity involves psychological/social elements, it is core to self and not easily changeable; for many, transition reflects authentic identity, not choice. (4) Political power: transgender individuals are a small, politically vulnerable minority. Recent cases (Grimm, Skrmetti) grapple with this classification, reflecting its quasi-suspect nature.

choices.text: Gender identity should not be a suspect classification because it is mutable and involves personal choice, unlike immutable characteristics like race or sex. Individuals can change their gender identity, so it fails the immutability prong, and political power analysis is irrelevant for non-suspect classifications.
choices.explanation: This answer conflates mutability with authenticity. The immutability factor does not require that characteristics be biologically unchangeable; it requires that they be core to identity and not easily alterable for practical or social purposes. Race and sex are suspect partly because they are immutable, but the test also applies to characteristics that are fundamental to identity even if theoretically mutable. Moreover, for many transgender individuals, gender identity is core and unchanging; the transition, not the identity, is the change. The argument that gender identity is 'choice' mischaracterizes a deep aspect of identity.

choices.text: Gender identity is not a suspect classification because the Court has never explicitly recognized it as such. Until the Supreme Court makes a clear pronouncement, lower courts must apply rational basis review.
choices.explanation: While it is true that the Supreme Court has not explicitly held gender identity to be suspect, lower courts have authority to apply the four-factor test and to recognize emerging suspect classifications. Courts regularly apply the test to novel classifications. Moreover, recent decisions (Grimm v. Gloucester County, U.S. v. Skrmetti) show the Supreme Court engaging with gender identity as a classification worthy of serious scrutiny, even if not explicitly labeled 'suspect.' Waiting for explicit Supreme Court pronouncement would paralyze lower courts.

choices.text: Gender identity is a suspect classification only if individuals undergo medical transition. Those without medical transition are not protected because their identity is not biologically real.
choices.explanation: This answer conflates the authenticity of identity with medical transition and inappropriately conditions constitutional protection on medical choice. Transgender identity exists independent of medical transition, and many transgender individuals do not pursue transition for medical, financial, or personal reasons. Constitutional protection cannot be conditioned on undergoing specific medical procedures. The statute at issue bars all amendments, regardless of transition status, suggesting that the classification targets gender identity itself, not medical status.

steps.title: Sex-Based Discrimination Analysis
steps.prompt: Could this statute alternatively be analyzed as sex-based discrimination triggering intermediate scrutiny under Craig v. Boren, and would that analysis yield a different result?

choices.text: Yes. The statute can be analyzed as sex-based discrimination because it draws lines based on sex (the statute bars only those whose sex designation differs from birth assignment, a sex-based classification). This triggers intermediate scrutiny requiring an important governmental objective and substantial relationship between the means and objective. While the state's interests (record integrity) may be important, a complete ban is not substantially related to that objective when alternatives (administrative notes) exist.
choices.explanation: Under United States v. Virginia and Frontiero v. Richardson, statutes drawing lines based on sex face intermediate scrutiny. This statute explicitly classifies based on sex (permitting amendment for some but not those transitioning), so it is facially sex-based. The state's legitimate interests in vital record accuracy do not require a total ban; states can maintain records through administrative notes or annotations. The complete prohibition is not substantially related to the legitimate objective. Intermediate scrutiny analysis parallels quasi-suspect classification doctrine and may provide an alternative ground for invalidity.

choices.text: No. The statute is not sex-based discrimination because it applies equally to all individuals; it does not distinguish between men and women. Everyone is prohibited from changing sex designation, so there is no sex-based classification.
choices.explanation: This answer misunderstands sex discrimination doctrine. Sex-based classifications can be facially neutral if they have a disparate impact on one sex. More relevantly, here the statute creates sex-based distinction by targeting individuals whose sex designation differs from their birth assignment—a category defined by reference to sex. Courts have recognized that restrictions on transition procedures target gender identity but implicitly classify based on sex. The facial neutrality of application does not preclude sex-based classification analysis.

choices.text: Yes, the statute is sex-based discrimination, but intermediate scrutiny is satisfied because the state's interest in vital record integrity is important, and the statute is substantially related to that interest.
choices.explanation: While the statute can be analyzed as sex-based, intermediate scrutiny is not satisfied. The state's interest in record integrity is important, but a complete prohibition is not substantially related to that objective. States can maintain accurate records through administrative annotations; many states permit sex designation changes while noting the change in records. The statute is both over-inclusive (preventing changes where integrity is not at stake) and under-inclusive (permitting other inaccuracies in vital records). Under-tailoring is fatal to intermediate scrutiny.

choices.text: No. The statute does not invoke sex as a classification and therefore cannot trigger sex discrimination analysis, which applies only to facially sex-based statutes.
choices.explanation: This answer conflates facial neutrality with absence of sex classification. A statute need not explicitly mention sex to classify based on sex. When a statute targets a group defined by reference to sex (those whose gender identity differs from sex assigned at birth), it is a sex-based classification even if not explicitly labeling sex. Courts regularly apply sex discrimination analysis to facially neutral statutes with sex-based effects or group definitions.

steps.title: Grimm and Skrmetti: Precedent and Trajectory
steps.prompt: How do Grimm v. Gloucester County School Board (4th Cir, 2020) and United States v. Skrmetti (6th Cir and Supreme Court decisions) inform whether this statute should be invalidated?

choices.text: Grimm recognized that discrimination based on transgender status is related to sex discrimination and applied a searching level of scrutiny to bathroom access policies. Although Skrmetti's Supreme Court decision upheld a state law barring transition-related care for minors, Skrmetti involved medical care access, not vital records. Grimm's framework—requiring careful scrutiny of classifications based on transgender status—applies here more clearly than Skrmetti's deference to regulation of medical access. The birth certificate ban may be distinguishable from Skrmetti as not directly regulating medical care.
choices.explanation: Grimm applied intermediate or heightened scrutiny to discrimination based on transgender status, recognizing the connection to sex discrimination. While Skrmetti (2024) provided some deference to medical regulations, the case involved regulating access to transition-related medical care for minors, a domain where states have traditional authority over healthcare. A birth certificate amendment statute is distinct because it affects vital records, not medical access. Courts may distinguish medical regulation (where Skrmetti deference applies) from administrative records (where Grimm's heightened scrutiny applies). The two cases reflect a doctrine still developing.

choices.text: Skrmetti's Supreme Court holding that states can restrict access to transition-related care establishes that courts must apply rational basis review to all transgender-related statutes. Grimm is superseded by Skrmetti, and this statute is constitutional under rational basis as substantially related to vital record integrity.
choices.explanation: While Skrmetti did uphold medical restrictions, it did not establish that all transgender-related statutes receive rational basis review. Skrmetti involved medical care, where states have traditional regulatory authority. The case does not necessarily extend to vital records, administrative status, or non-medical domains. Courts have distinguished medical regulation from other contexts. Moreover, Grimm, decided before Skrmetti, applied heightened scrutiny to bathroom access; courts may continue applying heightened scrutiny outside the medical context.

choices.text: Both Grimm and Skrmetti held that any statute affecting transgender individuals must be struck down as discrimination. Therefore, this birth certificate statute is unconstitutional.
choices.explanation: Neither Grimm nor Skrmetti holds that all transgender-related statutes are unconstitutional. Grimm applied heightened scrutiny and found discrimination. Skrmetti upheld medical restrictions while applying some level of scrutiny. Both cases reflect domain-specific analysis rather than categorical invalidation of all laws affecting transgender individuals. The trajectory is complex and contested.

choices.text: Grimm's holding applies only to school bathroom access and does not extend to vital records. Skrmetti controls all transgender-related statutes outside the school context, and Skrmetti permits states to restrict recognition of transgender identity, so the birth certificate ban is constitutional.
choices.explanation: This answer over-generalizes from Skrmetti while over-narrowing Grimm. Grimm's reasoning about discrimination based on transgender status and heightened scrutiny is not limited to bathrooms; it is a principle applicable to other domains. Skrmetti involved medical access, not vital records recognition. Neither case definitively settles whether a complete ban on amending vital records survives scrutiny, and courts must apply the principles from both cases to analyze the statute's constitutionality.

overallExplanation: This scenario tests application of the four-factor suspect classification test to gender identity, integration of sex discrimination doctrine, and synthesis of emerging Supreme Court and circuit precedent on transgender rights. It requires students to see how multiple doctrinal frameworks (suspect classification, sex discrimination, and specific transgender rights cases) inform constitutional analysis.
caseReferences: Grimm v. Gloucester County School Board, 972 F.3d 586 (4th Cir. 2020)
United States v. Skrmetti, 83 F.4th 641 (6th Cir. 2023), aff'd 602 U.S. 753 (2024)
professorNote: This advanced scenario engages with cutting-edge equal protection doctrine. It tests whether students can apply foundational suspect classification doctrine to emerging questions and integrate recent Supreme Court precedent. It also tests whether they can distinguish domains (medical care vs. vital records) and apply different levels of scrutiny accordingly.
otherSide: A formalist might argue that gender identity is not an immutable, historical classification warranting suspect status, that states have legitimate interests in maintaining accurate vital records keyed to biological sex, and that Skrmetti signals judicial deference to state regulation in this domain. A functionalist might argue that the complete ban serves no legitimate purpose, imposes severe burdens on transgender individuals, and reflects animus toward transgender people.

precedentConditions.descriptionAppend: Precedent in play: your earlier Geduldig ruling drew careful lines around sex-based classifications. The central question here — whether gender identity warrants suspect or quasi-suspect classification — requires you to define the boundaries of sex-based equal protection analysis.

stepModifications.textAppend: Your earlier precision about what counts as sex-based classification is directly relevant to the threshold question here.

precedentConditions.descriptionAppend: Precedent in play: your earlier expansive reading of sex-based classifications may support treating gender identity as a sex-linked classification warranting heightened review.

stepModifications.textAppend: Your broader approach to sex-linked equal protection analysis may favor recognizing gender identity as warranting heightened scrutiny.

title: The Right to Procreate Through Surrogacy: Glucksberg, Obergefell, and Unenumerated Rights After Dobbs
doctrineArea: Substantive Due Process - Unenumerated Rights
description: A state enacts a law banning all compensated surrogacy arrangements, prohibiting any woman from carrying a pregnancy for compensation and prohibiting any person from engaging a surrogate for compensation. The statute does permit altruistic surrogacy (unpaid gestational agreements) but makes it illegal for anyone to pay a surrogate or for a surrogate to accept payment. A single man in his fifties, biologically unable to procreate without a surrogate due to a congenital condition affecting sperm production, seeks to engage a surrogate to have a biological child with his genetic material. He challenges the statute as violating his fundamental right to procreate under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The state defends the ban as protecting women from exploitation in gestational labor and protecting children from commodification.

steps.title: Glucksberg's 'Deeply Rooted in History and Tradition' Test
steps.prompt: Under Washington v. Glucksberg's test for fundamental rights ('deeply rooted in history and tradition'), does the challenger have a fundamental right to procreate through compensated surrogacy?

choices.text: No. Under Glucksberg's historical test, compensated surrogacy does not qualify as a fundamental right. While the right to procreate is recognized (Skinner), the specific form of procreation through paid surrogacy is not deeply rooted in history and tradition. For most of history, surrogacy was either unknown, condemned, or heavily restricted. Glucksberg's rigid test requires recognition of specific practices, not abstract rights, so compensated surrogacy fails.
choices.explanation: Glucksberg explicitly requires that unenumerated rights be 'deeply rooted in the nation's history and tradition.' The majority used this formalist test to reject a right to physician-assisted suicide. Applied here, compensated surrogacy is a modern phenomenon not deeply rooted in historical practice. While procreation is fundamental, the specific practice of paid gestational surrogacy is not. Glucksberg's approach would likely exclude surrogacy because it is a novel practice. The test's formalism means it often excludes modern manifestations of liberty even if connected to recognized fundamental interests.

choices.text: Yes. The right to procreate, recognized in Skinner v. Oklahoma, is fundamental, and the specific means of procreation should not matter. Because compensated surrogacy enables procreation for those who cannot procreate naturally, it is a modern manifestation of the fundamental right to procreate and should be protected.
choices.explanation: While this answer appeals to modern understandings of liberty, it does not apply Glucksberg's test correctly. Glucksberg did not hold that fundamental rights extend automatically to modern manifestations; it held that the right must be deeply rooted in tradition, narrowly defined. The opinion explicitly rejected expanding rights based on modern technological possibilities. Skinner recognized the right to procreate in the context of preventing involuntary sterilization, not in the context of accessing surrogacy. Glucksberg's formalist approach would likely distinguish between the traditional right to procreate and the modern practice of compensated surrogacy.

choices.text: No. The right is not fundamental because a person can avoid the restriction by not engaging in surrogacy. The state is not preventing procreation; it is restricting one method of procreation. Glucksberg's test applies only to complete deprivations, not to restrictions on specific means.
choices.explanation: This answer misapplies the logic of fundamental rights doctrine. Even though alternatives exist, restrictions on procreation methods can implicate fundamental rights. For the plaintiff, surrogacy is not one among many equal options; it is the only biological procreation option given his medical condition. Fundamental rights doctrine does not require that the state eliminate all means of exercising a right; it permits states to burden some means, but not to eliminate the right for those for whom that means is essential. Still, Glucksberg's historical test would likely exclude surrogacy.

choices.text: Yes. Glucksberg's test has been effectively overruled by Obergefell v. Hodges, which holds that fundamental rights evolve to reflect modern understandings of liberty. Therefore, surrogacy is protected as a modern manifestation of the right to procreate.
choices.explanation: While Obergefell did take a more expansive approach to fundamental rights (discussed in the next step), it did not explicitly overrule Glucksberg's historical test. Obergefell acknowledged Glucksberg but used different reasoning. Glucksberg remains good law and is still cited by the Supreme Court in recent cases (Dobbs cited Glucksberg and approved its methodology). The relationship between Glucksberg and Obergefell is complex and contested, but it is inaccurate to say Glucksberg has been overruled.

steps.title: Obergefell's Alternative Approach
steps.prompt: How would Obergefell v. Hodges's approach to fundamental rights differ from Glucksberg, and would it lead to invalidation of the surrogacy ban?

choices.text: Obergefell's approach focuses on liberty interests, dignity, and the significance of a practice to individuals' life plans rather than requiring historical rootedness. Under Obergefell's framework, the right to procreate (including through surrogacy for those who cannot procreate naturally) is deeply significant to identity and autonomy. The burden on procreation (especially for one who cannot procreate otherwise) implicates fundamental liberty. Obergefell would likely protect surrogacy as part of reproductive autonomy.
choices.explanation: In Obergefell, Justice Kennedy emphasized dignity, autonomy, and the fundamental importance of relationships and family formation to individuals' conception of their identity. This approach differs from Glucksberg's requirement of historical rootedness. Under Obergefell's framework, the question becomes whether surrogacy is sufficiently important to individuals' identity and autonomy, not whether it is historically rooted. For someone biologically unable to procreate otherwise, surrogacy may be central to autonomy and identity. Obergefell's methodology would more readily protect surrogacy than Glucksberg's would.

choices.text: Obergefell does not provide a different approach; it applies the same historical-rootedness test as Glucksberg. Therefore, Obergefell would not change the analysis, and the surrogacy ban would still be constitutional.
choices.explanation: This answer mischaracterizes Obergefell. While Obergefell did cite Glucksberg, the opinion moved away from exclusive reliance on historical rootedness. Justice Kennedy's majority opinion in Obergefell emphasized four principles: (1) autonomy, (2) dignity, (3) relationships, and (4) recognition. These concepts reflect a more open-ended approach to fundamental rights than Glucksberg's historical test. Courts have recognized that Obergefell represents a different methodology.

choices.text: Obergefell applies heightened scrutiny to restrictions on marriage but does not establish a general fundamental right to autonomy. The surrogacy right would still fail under Obergefell because surrogacy is not a recognized fundamental right, and the statute can satisfy rational basis review.
choices.explanation: While Obergefell's holding directly concerned marriage, the opinion's reasoning about autonomy, dignity, and fundamental liberties extends beyond marriage. The opinion articulates principles about liberty that courts have applied to other domains. Moreover, Obergefell's framework for identifying fundamental interests is broader than rational basis review. The opinion would likely support a more searching analysis of the surrogacy restriction than rational basis would provide.

choices.text: Obergefell would protect surrogacy only if the plaintiff was married or in a legal relationship. For a single individual, even under Obergefell, there is no fundamental right to procreate through surrogacy.
choices.explanation: Obergefell's reasoning about dignity and autonomy is not limited to married persons. The opinion does emphasize the importance of relationships, but it articulates broader principles about autonomy and self-determination that are not limited to the married context. While Obergefell's core holding concerned marriage, its methodology could support protection of procreative autonomy for single individuals as well.

steps.title: Dobbs and the Post-Overruling Framework
steps.prompt: After Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization overruled Roe v. Wade, which Glucksberg or Obergefell approach controls for analyzing unenumerated rights like procreation through surrogacy?

choices.text: After Dobbs, Glucksberg's historical-rootedness test is controlling for unenumerated rights. Dobbs explicitly rejected Obergefell's methodology and emphasized that unenumerated rights must be 'deeply rooted in the nation's history and tradition' and 'narrowly tailored.' Under this stricter framework, surrogacy (not historically rooted) fails to qualify as a fundamental right, and the surrogacy ban likely satisfies rational basis review.
choices.explanation: In Dobbs, the Supreme Court explicitly returned to Glucksberg's historical-rootedness test as the controlling methodology for identifying unenumerated rights. The majority stated that Roe was wrongly decided partly because abortion was not deeply rooted in history and tradition. Dobbs's reasoning applies broadly to unenumerated rights, not just abortion. Obergefell is now characterized by some Justices as improperly extending fundamental rights beyond historical practice. Under Dobbs's framework, modern practices like surrogacy face a high bar to recognition as fundamental rights. This represents a significant shift toward Glucksberg's formalism.

choices.text: After Dobbs, Obergefell remains controlling for identifying unenumerated rights, and Dobbs did not affect the application of Obergefell's autonomy-based framework. Therefore, surrogacy would likely be protected as a fundamental liberty under Obergefell.
choices.explanation: While Dobbs did not explicitly overrule Obergefell, it signaled skepticism toward Obergefell's more expansive methodology. Several Justices in Dobbs criticized Obergefell-style approaches to fundamental rights, and the opinion emphasized Glucksberg's historical test. Courts have interpreted Dobbs as constraining Obergefell's application. It is unlikely that courts would apply Obergefell's autonomy framework to recognize surrogacy as fundamental after Dobbs.

choices.text: Dobbs created a new test balancing historical rootedness with modern autonomy interests. Courts must now apply a hybrid test combining Glucksberg and Obergefell approaches.
choices.explanation: Dobbs did not create a new hybrid test. The opinion explicitly endorsed Glucksberg's historical-rootedness methodology and rejected what it saw as the more permissive Obergefell approach. While courts may attempt to reconcile Glucksberg and Obergefell in limited contexts, Dobbs establishes Glucksberg as the primary framework for identifying unenumerated rights.

choices.text: Dobbs only addressed abortion rights and does not affect other unenumerated rights like procreation through surrogacy. Therefore, Obergefell's approach still controls for surrogacy.
choices.explanation: While Dobbs's holding directly concerned abortion, its reasoning about the methodology for identifying unenumerated rights applies broadly. The opinion's rejection of Roe rested on the principle that unenumerated rights must be deeply rooted in history, and this principle governs all unenumerated rights, not only abortion. Courts have applied Dobbs's reasoning beyond abortion to constrain other unenumerated rights claims.

overallExplanation: This scenario tests synthesis of three major substantive due process frameworks (Glucksberg, Obergefell, and Dobbs) and requires students to understand how the post-Dobbs constitutional landscape has shifted the methodology for identifying and protecting unenumerated rights. It demonstrates how competing methodologies can yield opposite results and how Supreme Court shifts in composition affect fundamental rights doctrine.
caseReferences: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, 142 S. Ct. 2228 (2022)
Skinner v. Oklahoma ex rel. Williamson, 316 U.S. 535 (1942)
Michael H. v. Gerald D., 491 U.S. 110 (1989)
professorNote: This advanced scenario tests the fundamental methodological divide in substantive due process doctrine. The source materials identify critical competing frameworks: (1) Washington v. Glucksberg's "deeply rooted in history and tradition" approach (restrictive, characteristic of recent doctrine); (2) Obergefell's broader formulation based on individual autonomy and fundamental liberty (expansive, used for marriage); (3) Dobbs's invocation of Glucksberg (suggesting a return to the narrow approach). Whether Obergefell implicitly overruled Glucksberg or whether the two coexist remains an open doctrinal question. Dobbs subsequently invoked Glucksberg as controlling, but the tension between these approaches is genuine and unresolved.
otherSide: A functionalist might argue that the right to procreate is fundamental and that modern manifestations (including surrogacy) should be protected, that Dobbs's rigid historicism denies liberty to those whose circumstances did not exist historically, and that autonomy and dignity (Obergefell principles) better capture constitutional values than formalist historical analysis.

precedentConditions.descriptionAppend: Precedent in play: your earlier embrace of the selective incorporation test — fundamental to ordered liberty and deeply rooted in history — frames the analysis here. Whether a right to surrogacy satisfies that demanding historical standard is the central question after Dobbs and Glucksberg.

stepModifications.textAppend: Your earlier endorsement of the deeply-rooted-in-history test applies with full force to this unenumerated-rights claim.

precedentConditions.descriptionAppend: Precedent in play: your earlier rejection of the selective incorporation framework suggests openness to a more flexible approach to unenumerated rights — but the Dobbs majority has since narrowed the path for recognizing new fundamental rights.

stepModifications.textAppend: Your earlier flexibility with incorporation analysis creates space for a broader rights inquiry, though Dobbs constrains the doctrinal landscape.

title: Abolishing the Grand Jury: Selective Incorporation and the Unincorporated Fifth Amendment Right
doctrineArea: Due Process - Incorporation of the Bill of Rights
description: A state enacts a new criminal procedure code that abolishes the grand jury indictment requirement for all criminal prosecutions, replacing it with a probable cause determination at a preliminary hearing conducted before a magistrate judge. Under the new procedure, prosecutors may proceed by filing an information (a prosecutorial charging document) in felony cases without obtaining a grand jury indictment, provided the prosecutor obtains a magistrate's finding of probable cause at a preliminary hearing. A defendant indicted by information (without grand jury proceedings) for a felony argues that the Fifth Amendment's Grand Jury Clause ('No person shall be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law') requires that all felony prosecutions be initiated by grand jury indictment. The defendant seeks to have the state charges dismissed.

steps.title: Selective Incorporation Test
steps.prompt: What is the test for selective incorporation of Bill of Rights provisions against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause?

choices.text: Under McDonald v. City of Chicago, a Bill of Rights provision is incorporated against the states if it is 'fundamental to our scheme of ordered liberty' or 'deeply rooted in the nation's history and tradition.' Courts apply this two-part test to determine whether the right is so essential to liberty that it must apply to states. If incorporated, the right applies to states with the same content and scope as it applies to the federal government.
choices.explanation: McDonald's selective incorporation test derives from Palko v. Connecticut and requires that incorporated rights be either 'fundamental to our scheme of ordered liberty' (Palko's test) or 'deeply rooted in the nation's history and tradition' (Duncan v. Louisiana's articulation). A right meeting either criterion is incorporated, meaning the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause makes it applicable to states. This test permits courts to examine whether a particular right is essential to ordered liberty. When incorporated, the right applies to states identically to its application to the federal government.

choices.text: The incorporation test requires only that a right be found in the Bill of Rights. All Bill of Rights provisions have been automatically incorporated against the states without case-by-case analysis.
choices.explanation: This answer conflates the scope of incorporation with the test. While most Bill of Rights provisions have been incorporated, incorporation has not been automatic. Courts conduct case-by-case analysis using the McDonald test. Some provisions (like the Grand Jury Clause) have been explicitly held not to satisfy the test. The Supreme Court must determine whether each right meets the fundamental liberty or historical rootedness criteria.

choices.text: The incorporation test examines whether a right is explicitly mentioned in the Fourteenth Amendment's text. Bill of Rights provisions that are textually incorporated in the Fourteenth Amendment apply to states; others do not.
choices.explanation: The Fourteenth Amendment does not explicitly incorporate specific Bill of Rights provisions by textual reference. The incorporation doctrine is judge-made law, not derived from explicit textual incorporation. The Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause is the vehicle for incorporation, but the clause does not specify which rights are included. Courts determine incorporation through the fundamental liberty / historical rootedness test, not through textual matching.

choices.text: The incorporation test requires that a right be essential to criminal procedure. Any procedural right in the Bill of Rights is automatically incorporated because procedure is fundamental.
choices.explanation: This answer conflates procedural nature with incorporability. While many procedural rights have been incorporated (including most of the Fifth, Sixth, and Eighth Amendments), not all procedural provisions satisfy the fundamental liberty test. The Grand Jury Clause is a procedural right explicitly held not to be fundamental. Courts examine each procedural right individually to determine if it is fundamental to ordered liberty, not assuming automatic incorporation of all procedural safeguards.

steps.title: Why the Grand Jury Clause is Not Incorporated
steps.prompt: Why has the Fifth Amendment's Grand Jury Clause NOT been incorporated against the states, despite incorporation of nearly every other criminal procedure provision?

choices.text: The Supreme Court held in Hurtado v. California that the Grand Jury Clause is not fundamental to ordered liberty. While the grand jury is a traditional Anglo-American institution, the Court determined that the grand jury is not essential to procedural due process because other procedures (like preliminary hearings and prosecutorial charging) can adequately protect defendants. Most states have abolished or minimized grand jury requirements without violating due process, showing the requirement is not fundamental.
choices.explanation: Hurtado v. California (1884) explicitly held that the Fifth Amendment's Grand Jury Clause is not incorporated. The Court reasoned that while the grand jury is traditional in federal practice, other procedures can serve the grand jury's protective functions. The Court noted that states have constitutional authority to adopt different charging procedures. Subsequently, courts have noted that many states use information procedures with preliminary hearings instead of grand juries, and this variation demonstrates that grand jury procedures are not fundamental to ordered liberty. The grand jury's role (screening weak cases) can be performed by other mechanisms.

choices.text: The Grand Jury Clause has not been incorporated because grand juries are slow and inefficient. The Supreme Court determined that due process does not require inefficient procedures, only effective ones.
choices.explanation: The incorporation test does not turn on efficiency. Many constitutional rights require less efficient procedures (trials, counsel, appeals). The Supreme Court's reasoning was not about efficiency but about whether the grand jury is fundamental to protecting liberty. While the Court acknowledged practical concerns, the core holding was that grand jury procedures are not essential to due process.

choices.text: The Grand Jury Clause has not been incorporated because it is not mentioned in the Fourteenth Amendment's text, and incorporation requires explicit textual basis.
choices.explanation: No Bill of Rights provision is explicitly textually incorporated in the Fourteenth Amendment. The incorporation doctrine is based on the Due Process Clause, which does not specify particular rights. The Grand Jury Clause's absence from incorporation is based on the Hurtado holding that it is not fundamental, not on textual considerations.

choices.text: The Grand Jury Clause has not been incorporated because the Supreme Court has never addressed the question. Lower courts therefore cannot determine incorporation status.
choices.explanation: The Supreme Court directly addressed grand jury incorporation in Hurtado v. California (1884). The Court explicitly held that the clause is not incorporated. This holding remains controlling law, reaffirmed in modern doctrine. It is not an unresolved question.

overallExplanation: This scenario tests understanding of the selective incorporation doctrine, the test for determining which Bill of Rights provisions apply to states, and application of that test to the Grand Jury Clause. It demonstrates that incorporation is not automatic and that courts must assess each provision's relationship to ordered liberty.
caseReferences: McDonald v. City of Chicago, 561 U.S. 742 (2010)
professorNote: This scenario teaches incorporation doctrine. Key distinction from source materials: Nearly all Bill of Rights protections have been incorporated through selective incorporation, with principal exceptions being the Seventh Amendment (civil jury trial) and Fifth Amendment (grand jury indictment). So the grand jury requirement specifically has not been incorporated and remains a rare exception. The scenario demonstrates why applicability to states is not automatic and why incorporation analysis remains doctrinally relevant despite its widespread success.
otherSide: A functionalist might argue that the grand jury has unique protective value (protecting against prosecutorial overreach through citizen participation), that modern trends toward abolishing grand juries do not establish lack of fundamentality, and that the right to a grand jury should be incorporated despite its disuse.

title: The Heartbeat Act After Dobbs: Rational Basis and the End of Constitutional Abortion Rights
doctrineArea: Substantive Due Process - Abortion Post-Dobbs
description: A state passes a 'Heartbeat Act' banning abortion after 6 weeks (before most women know they're pregnant), with exceptions only for life-threatening medical emergencies. Criminal penalties of up to 10 years for physicians who perform prohibited abortions. A physician and clinic challenge the law as violating constitutional liberty interests.

steps.title: Standard of Review After Dobbs
steps.prompt: After Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization overruled Roe v. Wade, what standard of review applies to state abortion restrictions?

choices.text: Rational basis review—the state merely needs to show a rational relationship to a legitimate state interest.
choices.explanation: Dobbs explicitly held that abortion is not a liberty interest protected by the Fourteenth Amendment, eliminating strict scrutiny and restoring rational basis review. The state may regulate abortion to protect potential life from conception without meeting heightened scrutiny. This represents a complete doctrinal shift from the trimester framework of Roe v. Wade and the undue burden standard of Planned Parenthood v. Casey.

choices.text: Strict scrutiny—the state must demonstrate a compelling interest and use the least restrictive means.
choices.explanation: Strict scrutiny applied under the regime of Roe and Casey, which recognized abortion as a fundamental right. Dobbs explicitly rejected this framework, stating that the Constitution does not confer a general right to abortion. Strict scrutiny is now inapplicable to abortion regulations.

choices.text: Undue burden review—the law may not place substantial obstacles in the path of someone seeking an abortion before viability.
choices.explanation: The undue burden standard, established in Planned Parenthood v. Casey, is no longer applicable after Dobbs. Dobbs overruled Casey along with Roe, explicitly rejecting the premise that abortion constitutes a fundamental right protected by the Due Process Clause.

choices.text: Intermediate scrutiny—the state must demonstrate an important interest and use means substantially related to that interest.
choices.explanation: While intermediate scrutiny applies to some gender-based classifications and content-neutral speech restrictions, Dobbs clearly established that rational basis review applies to abortion restrictions. The state's interest in potential life is sufficient; no heightened scrutiny applies.

steps.title: Challenging the Life-Only Exception Under Rational Basis
steps.prompt: Can the challengers successfully argue that the life-only exception is unconstitutionally underinclusive (protecting potential life but not maternal health) under rational basis review?

choices.text: Yes, because underinclusiveness is a per se violation of rational basis review.
choices.explanation: Rational basis review traditionally tolerates substantial underinclusiveness. Courts have long held that legislatures may address problems incrementally and need not solve every related problem simultaneously. A law that protects life but not maternal health does not inherently fail rational basis review based on underinclusiveness alone, as demonstrated in cases like Williamson v. Lee Optical.

choices.text: No, because rational basis review presumes the law is constitutional and rarely strikes down legislation, and Dobbs explicitly held that protecting potential life is a legitimate interest.
choices.explanation: Under rational basis review, legislatures receive extreme deference. Once Dobbs established that the state has a legitimate interest in protecting potential life, the specific contours of that protection (even if underinclusive relative to maternal health) remain within legislative judgment. Rational basis review does not require perfect matching between ends and means, and Dobbs provides clear state authority to protect potential life throughout pregnancy.

choices.text: Yes, because the Privileges and Immunities Clause protects the right to travel to other states seeking abortion.
choices.explanation: While some scholars have argued that abortion restrictions might implicate the right to interstate travel, Dobbs does not rely on this theory and courts have not adopted it. The case is grounded in the denial of a substantive due process right to abortion, not in privileges and immunities doctrine.

choices.text: Maybe, but only if challengers can prove the legislature was motivated by animus toward women, triggering heightened scrutiny.
choices.explanation: Even if a discriminatory purpose could be shown, Dobbs's framework does not provide a pathway to heightened scrutiny for abortion restrictions. The opinion gives the state broad authority to regulate abortion, and rational basis review does not typically examine legislative motivation for economic or social regulations.
